stem: The acoustic impedance of a tissue is determined by:
options: Density × speed of sound in that tissue
rationale: Acoustic impedance (Z) = density × propagation speed. Differences in Z at tissue interfaces determine reflection.

stem: Attenuation of ultrasound in soft tissue is approximately:
rationale: The average attenuation coefficient in soft tissue is approximately 0.5 dB per cm per MHz. Higher frequency = more attenuation.

stem: The period of an ultrasound wave is the:
rationale: Period is the time it takes for one complete cycle to occur. It is the reciprocal of frequency (period = 1/frequency).

stem: Snell's law describes:
options: Refraction of sound at an interface when the beam strikes at an oblique angle
rationale: Snell's law governs refraction — the change in direction of a sound beam when crossing an interface at a non-normal angle between media with different propagation speeds.

stem: The piezoelectric effect is responsible for:
options: Converting electrical energy to sound and sound back to electrical energy
rationale: Piezoelectric crystals convert electrical signals to mechanical (sound) energy (transmit) and mechanical energy back to electrical signals (receive).

stem: A phased array transducer is best suited for:
options: Cardiac imaging through small acoustic windows like intercostal spaces
rationale: Phased arrays use electronic steering from a small footprint, ideal for cardiac imaging through intercostal spaces where acoustic access is limited.

stem: Curvilinear transducers are preferred for abdominal scanning because:
options: They provide a wide field of view at depth with good tissue contact on curved surfaces
They have the highest frequency available
They are the smallest transducers available
rationale: Curvilinear (convex) transducers provide a wide field of view that expands with depth, ideal for abdominal organs while maintaining good surface contact.

stem: The bandwidth of a transducer refers to:
options: The range of frequencies the transducer can operate at
The physical width of the transducer
rationale: Bandwidth is the range of frequencies a transducer can emit and receive. Broadband transducers have wider bandwidth, allowing better axial resolution and harmonic imaging.

stem: Endocavitary transducers typically operate at which frequency range?
rationale: Endocavitary transducers (transvaginal, transrectal) operate at 5-10 MHz since they are positioned close to target structures, requiring less penetration but higher resolution.

stem: The Doppler equation relates frequency shift to:
options: Blood flow velocity, transducer frequency, and angle of insonation
rationale: Doppler shift = (2 × velocity × transducer frequency × cos θ) / speed of sound. Velocity, frequency, and angle all affect the measured shift.

stem: Aliasing in pulsed wave Doppler occurs when:
options: The Doppler shift exceeds the Nyquist limit (half the PRF)
The transducer frequency is too low
The gain is set too high
rationale: Aliasing occurs when the sampled Doppler shift exceeds the Nyquist limit (PRF/2). The signal wraps around and appears to flow in the opposite direction.

stem: To reduce aliasing, the sonographer can:
options: Increase the PRF (scale), lower the baseline, or use CW Doppler
rationale: Increasing PRF raises the Nyquist limit. Baseline shift accommodates unidirectional flow. CW Doppler has no Nyquist limit and eliminates aliasing.

stem: Color Doppler displays:
options: Mean velocity and direction of blood flow overlaid on B-mode image
rationale: Color Doppler shows mean velocity (brightness) and direction (red toward/blue away by convention) superimposed on the B-mode image.

stem: The Doppler angle that produces NO detectable Doppler shift is:
rationale: At 90°, cos(90°) = 0, so the Doppler equation yields zero shift. No flow component is detected when the beam is perpendicular to flow.

stem: Power Doppler differs from color Doppler in that it:
options: Displays the amplitude/strength of the Doppler signal rather than velocity or direction
rationale: Power Doppler maps signal amplitude (number of moving red cells), not velocity or direction. It is more sensitive to slow flow but is angle-independent.

stem: A reverberation artifact appears as:
options: Equally spaced parallel lines deep to a strong reflector
A shadow behind a calcified structure
An area of increased echogenicity behind a cyst
A curved bright line in the far field
rationale: Reverberations occur when sound bounces between two strong reflectors, creating equally spaced parallel echoes at progressively deeper positions.

stem: Posterior acoustic shadowing is seen deep to:
rationale: Shadowing occurs when sound is reflected or absorbed by dense/calcified structures (stones, calcifications), preventing transmission to deeper tissues.

stem: Posterior acoustic enhancement occurs deep to:
rationale: Enhancement (through-transmission) occurs behind fluid because sound passes through with less attenuation than surrounding tissue, making deeper echoes brighter.

stem: A mirror artifact is most commonly seen around:
options: The diaphragm, causing hepatic structures to appear in the thorax
rationale: Mirror artifact occurs at strong curved reflectors like the diaphragm. Liver structures appear duplicated on the thoracic side.

stem: Edge shadowing (refraction artifact) appears at:
options: The curved lateral margins of cysts or round structures
rationale: Edge shadowing occurs at curved interfaces where sound refracts, creating narrow shadows at the lateral edges of round structures.

stem: The normal liver echogenicity compared to the right kidney cortex is:
rationale: Normal liver is slightly more echogenic than the renal cortex. Fatty liver shows increased echogenicity. Cirrhosis may show heterogeneous echotexture.

stem: A simple hepatic cyst on ultrasound demonstrates:
options: Anechoic content, smooth thin walls, posterior acoustic enhancement
rationale: Simple cysts are anechoic (black), have imperceptibly thin smooth walls, and demonstrate posterior acoustic enhancement. No internal echoes or septations.

stem: The portal vein is distinguished from hepatic veins because it has:
options: Echogenic walls (due to surrounding connective tissue)
rationale: Portal veins have echogenic/bright walls due to surrounding Glisson's capsule and connective tissue. Hepatic veins have thin, non-echogenic walls.

stem: Hepatic hemangioma typically appears as:
options: Well-defined hyperechoic (bright) mass with posterior acoustic enhancement
rationale: Cavernous hemangiomas are the most common benign hepatic tumors, typically appearing as well-defined, homogeneous, hyperechoic masses with posterior enhancement.

stem: The hepatic veins drain into:
rationale: The three hepatic veins (right, middle, left) converge and drain into the IVC near the diaphragm. They carry blood from the liver sinusoids.

stem: Fatty infiltration of the liver sonographically shows:
options: Increased echogenicity with decreased visualization of deep structures and vessels
rationale: Fatty liver shows diffusely increased echogenicity, poor penetration to deep structures, and decreased visualization of hepatic vessels and diaphragm.

stem: Classic sonographic findings of acute cholecystitis include:
options: Gallstones, positive sonographic Murphy sign, gallbladder wall thickening >3mm, pericholecystic fluid
rationale: Acute cholecystitis triad: gallstones with positive Murphy sign (maximal tenderness with transducer pressure over GB), wall thickening (>3mm), and pericholecystic fluid.

stem: The normal common bile duct (CBD) diameter is:
rationale: Normal CBD is ≤6mm. Post-cholecystectomy, up to 10mm may be normal. Elderly patients may have mildly dilated ducts without obstruction.

stem: A WES (wall-echo-shadow) sign indicates:
options: A gallbladder completely filled with stones
rationale: WES sign shows three layers: gallbladder wall, echogenic stone surface, and posterior shadow. It indicates a contracted or stone-packed gallbladder.

stem: Gallbladder polyps differ from gallstones because polyps:
options: Do not demonstrate posterior acoustic shadowing and do not move with position change
rationale: Polyps are attached to the GB wall, do not shadow (unless large), and do not move with patient repositioning. Stones shadow and are gravity-dependent.

stem: Choledocholithiasis is best identified by:
options: An echogenic focus within the CBD with posterior acoustic shadowing
rationale: Choledocholithiasis shows echogenic stone(s) within the CBD, typically with shadowing. CBD dilation (>6mm) upstream to the stone is often present.

stem: Hydronephrosis on ultrasound appears as:
options: Dilation of the renal pelvis and calyces with anechoic fluid
Increased echogenicity of the renal cortex
rationale: Hydronephrosis shows fluid-filled (anechoic) dilated collecting system. Graded as mild, moderate, or severe based on degree of calyceal dilation and cortical thinning.

stem: A renal calculus typically demonstrates:
options: Echogenic focus with posterior acoustic shadowing
Color Doppler flow within the lesion
rationale: Renal stones appear as bright echogenic foci that cast posterior acoustic shadows. Twinkling artifact on color Doppler can help confirm presence.

stem: The normal renal cortex should be:
options: Equal to or slightly less echogenic than the liver
Highly echogenic like renal sinus fat
rationale: Normal renal cortex is isoechoic to slightly hypoechoic compared to normal liver. Cortex brighter than liver suggests fatty liver or renal disease.

stem: A simple renal cyst meets all criteria EXCEPT:
rationale: Simple cysts must be anechoic, have thin smooth walls, and show enhancement. Internal echoes or septations make it complex, requiring further evaluation (Bosniak classification).

stem: Renal cell carcinoma most commonly appears sonographically as:
options: A solid mass that may be hypo-, iso-, or hyperechoic to the renal cortex
rationale: RCC can have variable echogenicity. Small tumors tend to be hyperechoic, larger ones may be heterogeneous. Internal vascularity on Doppler helps differentiate from cysts.

stem: The earliest ultrasound finding of intrauterine pregnancy is:
options: Gestational sac within the endometrium at 4.5-5 weeks
rationale: The gestational sac is the earliest sonographic sign of pregnancy, visible as early as 4.5-5 weeks gestational age transvaginally.

stem: A yolk sac should be visible within the gestational sac by:
options: 5.5 weeks transvaginally, or when the mean sac diameter is 8mm
rationale: The yolk sac is the first structure seen within the gestational sac, typically by 5.5 weeks TV or when MSD reaches 8mm. Its absence at expected timing raises concern.

stem: An ectopic pregnancy should be suspected when:
options: Adnexal mass with empty uterus and positive pregnancy test
Intrauterine gestational sac with yolk sac
rationale: An adnexal mass (especially a ring-like structure) combined with empty uterus and positive beta-hCG strongly suggests ectopic pregnancy.

stem: The discriminatory level of beta-hCG for transvaginal ultrasound is approximately:
rationale: At beta-hCG of 1,500-2,000 mIU/mL, a gestational sac should be visible on TVUS. Absence suggests ectopic or failed pregnancy.

stem: Crown-rump length (CRL) is most accurate for dating at:
rationale: CRL is most accurate in early first trimester (6-9 weeks), with accuracy of ±3-5 days. Accuracy decreases as gestational age increases.

stem: The biophysical profile (BPP) assesses which parameters?
options: Fetal breathing, movement, tone, amniotic fluid volume, and NST
rationale: BPP evaluates five parameters: fetal breathing movements, gross body movements, fetal tone, amniotic fluid volume (each scored 0 or 2), and non-stress test. Total score out of 10.

stem: Polyhydramnios is defined as AFI greater than:
rationale: Polyhydramnios (excess amniotic fluid) is defined as AFI >24cm or single deepest pocket >8cm. Associated with GDM, fetal anomalies, multiple gestations.

stem: Oligohydramnios is defined as AFI less than:
rationale: Oligohydramnios (decreased amniotic fluid) is AFI <5cm or single deepest pocket <2cm. Associated with IUGR, renal anomalies, post-dates pregnancy.

stem: Placenta previa is diagnosed when:
options: The placenta covers or is within 2cm of the internal cervical os
The placenta is in the fundus
rationale: Placenta previa occurs when the placenta partially or completely covers the internal cervical os. Diagnosed on TVUS. Risk of hemorrhage.

stem: Second trimester fetal anatomy survey should include all EXCEPT:
rationale: Standard anatomy survey includes BPD, head circumference, abdominal circumference, femur length, cerebellum, spine, kidneys, stomach, bladder, and 4-chamber heart. Lung density is not standard.

stem: The internal carotid artery (ICA) is distinguished from the external carotid artery (ECA) by:
options: Low-resistance flow pattern with continuous diastolic flow
High-resistance flow pattern with little diastolic flow
rationale: ICA has low-resistance flow (high diastolic flow) supplying the brain. ECA has high-resistance flow (little diastolic flow) supplying face/scalp. ICA is typically larger and posterolateral.

stem: An ICA stenosis of 70-99% typically shows peak systolic velocity (PSV) of:
rationale: 70-99% ICA stenosis: PSV >230 cm/s, EDV >100 cm/s, ICA/CCA ratio >4. These criteria are based on the Society of Radiologists in Ultrasound consensus.

stem: The ICA/CCA peak systolic velocity ratio helps assess:
options: Degree of internal carotid stenosis relative to the patient's systemic flow
rationale: ICA/CCA PSV ratio normalizes for patient-specific hemodynamics. Ratio >2 suggests ≥50% stenosis. Ratio >4 suggests ≥70% stenosis.

stem: Temporal tap test helps identify:
options: The external carotid artery by showing oscillations when tapping the temporal artery
rationale: Tapping the superficial temporal artery (ECA branch) produces visible oscillations in the ECA waveform, confirming vessel identity when anatomy is confusing.

stem: The primary diagnostic criterion for DVT is:
options: Non-compressibility of the vein with transducer pressure
rationale: Non-compressibility is the gold standard criterion. A normal vein collapses completely with gentle transducer pressure. DVT prevents complete compression.

stem: Augmentation in the venous system refers to:
options: Increased flow velocity with distal compression of the limb
rationale: Augmentation test: squeezing the calf or foot should produce a surge of venous flow. Absent or reduced augmentation suggests obstruction between the compression site and the probe.

stem: Chronic DVT differs from acute DVT in that chronic DVT shows:
options: Thrombus that is echogenic, irregular, with possible recanalization channels
rationale: Chronic DVT: echogenic/heterogeneous thrombus, irregular walls, thickened vein, possible recanalization channels. Acute: anechoic/hypoechoic, distended vein, smooth fresh thrombus.

stem: Superficial thrombophlebitis of the great saphenous vein near the saphenofemoral junction is significant because:
options: It can propagate into the deep venous system causing DVT/PE
rationale: SVT near the SFJ (within 3cm) has high risk of propagation into the common femoral vein, potentially causing DVT and pulmonary embolism.

stem: Features suggesting thyroid malignancy include:
options: Hypoechoic solid nodule with microcalcifications, irregular margins, and taller-than-wide shape
rationale: Suspicious features (TI-RADS): solid composition, hypoechoic, microcalcifications, irregular margins, taller-than-wide shape. Spongiform and purely cystic are benign features.

stem: A thyroid nodule described as 'spongiform' is:
options: Composed of multiple microcystic components resembling a sponge, suggesting benignity
rationale: Spongiform nodules (≥50% composed of small cystic spaces) are strongly associated with benign pathology (colloid nodules). FNA is generally not indicated.

stem: The ACR TI-RADS scoring system classifies thyroid nodules based on:
options: Composition, echogenicity, shape, margin, and echogenic foci
rationale: TI-RADS uses five ultrasound categories: composition, echogenicity, shape, margin, and echogenic foci. Points determine the category and FNA threshold.

stem: Hashimoto thyroiditis sonographically appears as:
options: Diffusely heterogeneous, hypoechoic gland with fibrous septations (micronodular pattern)
rationale: Hashimoto's shows diffuse heterogeneity with hypoechoic parenchyma and echogenic septations creating a 'micronodular' pattern. The gland may be enlarged or atrophied.

stem: Testicular torsion on ultrasound shows:
options: Absent or decreased intratesticular blood flow on color Doppler
rationale: Testicular torsion: absent or markedly decreased intratesticular blood flow compared to the asymptomatic side. Time-critical diagnosis. Surgical emergency.

stem: A hypoechoic intratesticular mass in a young male is most concerning for:
rationale: Any solid intratesticular mass in a young male is considered malignant until proven otherwise. Seminoma is the most common testicular neoplasm and typically appears hypoechoic.

stem: A varicocele is diagnosed when:
options: Dilated tortuous veins (>3mm) in the pampiniform plexus with flow augmentation during Valsalva
rationale: Varicocele: dilated pampiniform plexus veins >3mm with retrograde flow on Valsalva. More common on the left side due to left gonadal vein anatomy.

stem: Axial resolution is determined primarily by:
options: Spatial pulse length (shorter = better axial resolution)
rationale: Axial resolution = SPL/2. Shorter pulse length (higher frequency, fewer cycles) provides better axial resolution. It resolves structures along the beam axis.

stem: Lateral resolution is determined by:
options: Beam width at the depth of interest
rationale: Lateral resolution = beam width. Narrower beam = better lateral resolution. It is best at the focal zone where beam width is narrowest.

stem: Elevational (slice thickness) resolution is determined by:
options: Height of the transducer element in the elevation plane
rationale: Elevational resolution depends on element height perpendicular to the scan plane. It is fixed in conventional transducers. 1.5D arrays improve it.

stem: At the focal zone of a transducer:
options: Lateral resolution is best because the beam is narrowest
rationale: At the focal zone, the beam converges to its narrowest width, providing the best lateral resolution. Structures at this depth are resolved most clearly.

stem: Laminar flow in a vessel is characterized by:
options: A parabolic velocity profile with fastest flow in the center
rationale: Laminar flow has an organized parabolic profile: fastest at center, slowest near walls. Seen in normal straight vessel segments.

stem: Spectral broadening on Doppler waveforms suggests:
options: Disturbed or turbulent flow, often seen at stenosis
rationale: Spectral broadening (filling in of the spectral window) indicates disturbed flow with multiple velocities. Seen at sites of stenosis, branch points, or after a stenosis.

stem: Resistive index (RI) is calculated as:
options: (Peak systolic velocity - End diastolic velocity) / Peak systolic velocity
rationale: RI = (PSV - EDV) / PSV. Normal renal RI is <0.70. Elevated RI suggests increased downstream resistance (renal disease, obstruction).

stem: Pulsatility index (PI) formula:
rationale: PI = (PSV - EDV) / TAMx. PI accounts for the entire waveform morphology. Higher PI = more pulsatile/higher resistance flow.

stem: Focal nodular hyperplasia (FNH) characteristically demonstrates:
options: A central scar with spoke-wheel pattern vascularity on Doppler
rationale: FNH typically shows a central scar (may be echogenic or hypoechoic) with characteristic spoke-wheel arterial pattern on color Doppler. It is a benign lesion.

stem: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) most commonly develops in:
rationale: HCC develops predominantly in cirrhotic livers. Surveillance with ultrasound every 6 months is recommended for cirrhotic patients. May appear as focal mass or diffuse infiltration.

stem: A hepatic abscess typically appears as:
options: Complex cystic mass with internal debris, thick irregular walls, and possible gas echoes
rationale: Hepatic abscess: complex cystic mass with internal echoes/debris, thick irregular walls, and possibly gas (bright echoes with dirty shadowing). Clinical correlation essential.

stem: The pancreas is best visualized using which acoustic window?
options: Stomach or left lobe of liver as acoustic windows
rationale: The pancreas is retroperitoneal. The fluid-filled stomach or left hepatic lobe serves as acoustic windows. Patient positioning and water ingestion can improve visualization.

stem: Acute pancreatitis sonographically may show:
options: Enlarged hypoechoic gland with peripancreatic fluid collections
rationale: Acute pancreatitis: diffusely enlarged, hypoechoic (edematous) gland with possible peripancreatic fluid. However, ultrasound may appear normal in early/mild cases. CT is more sensitive.

stem: A pancreatic pseudocyst appears as:
options: Well-defined anechoic or hypoechoic fluid collection adjacent to or within the pancreas
rationale: Pseudocyst: encapsulated fluid collection without epithelial lining, typically seen after pancreatitis. Well-defined, round/oval, anechoic or with internal debris.

stem: Normal adult spleen length should not exceed:
rationale: Normal spleen length is up to 12-13 cm in adults. Splenomegaly is >13 cm. Causes include portal hypertension, infection, hematologic disorders.

stem: Splenic infarction appears sonographically as:
options: Wedge-shaped hypoechoic area at the periphery of the spleen
rationale: Splenic infarcts are typically wedge-shaped, hypoechoic areas at the splenic periphery with the base toward the capsule. They may become echogenic/calcify over time.

stem: During an ultrasound examination, the sonographer identifies a significant abnormality. The appropriate action is to:
options: Complete the exam thoroughly and communicate findings to the supervising physician
Tell the patient the diagnosis immediately
Stop the exam and send the patient home
rationale: Sonographers should not provide diagnoses to patients. Findings must be communicated to the interpreting physician who makes the diagnosis and communicates with the patient.

stem: Ergonomic guidelines for sonographers recommend:
options: Keeping the scanning arm close to the body, using proper posture, and taking regular breaks
Reaching across the patient's body for scanning
Twisting the torso to view the monitor
Applying maximum transducer pressure for best images
rationale: Proper ergonomics prevents work-related musculoskeletal disorders (WRMSDs): arm close to body, neutral posture, adjustable equipment, regular breaks, minimal pressure.

stem: The ALARA principle in ultrasound means:
options: Using the lowest output power and shortest exam time to obtain diagnostic information
Always scanning at maximum power for best image quality
Using thermal index settings above recommended levels
rationale: ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) applies to ultrasound: minimize output power, exposure time, and mechanical/thermal indices while maintaining diagnostic quality.
